mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the Balfour Declaration, Jewish settlement, partition of Palestine, establishment of Israel, Arab-Israeli wars, the intifada, and peace processes in B.1.c and B.3.c.

Iranian civilization from ancient Persia through the 1979 Islamic Revolution directly addresses Iran since 1925 (C.1), the regime of Reza Shah (C.1.a), Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's regime including the Mosaddeq crisis (C.1.b), and the revolution and Islamic Republic under Khomeini (C.1.c).

The 1991 Gulf War, 2003 invasion, Saddam Hussein's regime, and the rise of ISIS directly address Iraq's revolution of 1958, the Ba'th revolution, Iran-Iraq war, invasion of Kuwait, and defeat in the Persian Gulf War (B.3.d).

African history from prehistoric origins through colonial conquest, independence movements, and contemporary Africa broadly addresses the entire sub-Saharan Africa section (E), including colonial administration (E.1, E.2), nationalist movements and independence (E.3), and regional developments (E.4-E.8).

Political systems, governance, patronage networks, and colonial legacies in sub-Saharan Africa address the rise of African nationalist parties and establishment of independent countries (E.3), and political developments across West Africa (E.4), East Africa (E.6), Central Africa (E.7), and Southern Africa (E.8).

Mandela's role in the African National Congress, imprisonment, and presidency directly address antiapartheid movements, growing international isolation, legalization of the ANC, and establishment of black majority rule in 1994 (E.8.b.i).

Islam's divisions, Sharia law, the Ottoman Empire, and militant Islam connect to radicalization of Egyptian politics (B.3.e), the Arabian Peninsula and Saudi Arabia (B.4), Iran's Islamic Republic (C.1.c), and North Africa's Islamic fundamentalist movements (D).

Islamic civilization from the Arabian Peninsula through medieval caliphates to the modern era, Arab nationalism, and political Islam connect to Arab lands under the mandate system (B.1), the Arab states (B.3), the Arabian Peninsula (B.4), and North Africa (D).
Islamic jurisprudence across the Middle East and Africa connects to Saudi Arabia's governance under Islamic law (B.4.a) and Iran's Islamic Republic and Sharia-based governance (C.1.c).

Islamic fundamentalism connects to the rise of Islamic fundamentalism in Egypt (B.3.e), Iran's Islamic Republic under Khomeini (C.1.c), the rise of Islamic fundamentalists in Tunisia (D.2.b), and growing influence of Islamic fundamentalism in Algeria (D.2.d).

History of Zionism from Herzl through the creation of Israel in 1948 and the Six-Day War directly addresses the Balfour Declaration, Jewish settlement, and emergence of Israel (B.1.c) and the establishment of Israel and wars with Arab states (B.3.c.i).
Judaism including the establishment of Israel and the Holocaust connects to the acceleration of Jewish settlement in Palestine (B.1.c) and the establishment and development of Israel (B.3.c.i).

Transition from colonial empires to independent nation-states, Nehru and Nkrumah as independence leaders, addresses Arab lands under the mandate system (B.1), emergence of independent North African states (D), and decline of the colonial system in sub-Saharan Africa (E.3).

British imperial administration and decolonization connect to Turkey and Cyprus under British administration (A), Arab lands under the British mandate (B.1), British colonial rule in North Africa (D.1), and British colonial administration in sub-Saharan Africa (E.1, E.2).

British, Dutch, and French colonial empires and theories of colonialism connect to the mandate system in the Arab lands (B.1), French and Italian colonial rule in North Africa (D.1), and European colonial powers in sub-Saharan Africa (E.1, E.2).
Postcolonial experiences in Africa and the Middle East connect to the emergence of independent North African states (D) and nationalist movements and independence in sub-Saharan Africa (E).

Nationalism and self-determination connect to the development of the Turkish nation under Ataturk (A.1) and the rise of African nationalist parties and establishment of independent African countries (E.3).
Intrastate armed conflicts connect to the Lebanese civil wars (B.3.a), Somali clan-based civil war (E.6.b.i), the Congo's civil strife (E.7.a.i), and civil wars in Angola and Mozambique (E.8.b.vi).
Modern warfare including guerrilla tactics connects to the Iran-Iraq war (C.1.c) and guerrilla warfare in Angola and Mozambique (E.8.b.vi).
Terrorism and political violence connect to increasing civil strife in Algeria from Islamic fundamentalist and state violence (D.2.d).
Human rights frameworks and anti-discrimination connect to government-sanctioned apartheid and antiapartheid movements in South Africa (E.8.b.i).
Racism as structural and institutional phenomenon, including colonial racism and Cecil Rhodes, connects to apartheid in South Africa and political discrimination against black Africans (E.8.b.i).
Forced migration and refugee crises connect to the influx of refugees in Somalia (E.6.b.i) and the multinational intervention that followed clan-based civil war and famine.
Communism and socialist state development connect to the establishment of the military government and development of a Socialist state under Mengistu in Ethiopia (E.5.a).
Democracy and democratic transitions connect to Turkey's return to civilian government and constitutional reforms (A.3.c) and South Africa's first all-race elections in 1994 (E.8.b.i).
Authoritarian regimes connect to Ataturk's one-party government (A.2), military coups and one-party states in West Africa (E.4.b.ii), and apartheid-era South African authoritarianism (E.8.b.i).
Revolutionary theory addresses the Egyptian revolution of 1952 and Nasser's rise to power (B.3.e) and the Iranian revolution of 1978-79 and establishment of the Islamic Republic (C.1.c).
Fascism's manifestations connect to Libya under the Fascist Italian government from 1922 to 1942 (D.1.b).

Cold War superpower rivalry and proxy wars connect to the U.S.-sponsored coup against Mosaddeq in Iran (C.1.b), superpower involvement in Somalia (E.6.b.i), and Cuban troop intervention in Angola (E.8.b.vi).
UN peacekeeping in the Congo, intervention in Somalia, sanctions against Libya and South Africa, and the mandate system are all discussed.
Diplomatic history connects to Turkey's war for independence and nation-building (A.1) and Israeli-Arab diplomacy including the treaty with Egypt (B.3.c.i).
International law, the UN system, and sovereignty connect to the mandate system in Arab lands (B.1) and Namibia's UN mandate and international resistance to South African rule (E.8.b.ii).
World War I and the Paris Peace Settlement provide context for the Turkish war of independence (A.1) and the post-World War I mandate system in the Arab lands (B.1).

World War II connects to Egypt's participation (B.2), Libya under Italian rule and British occupation (D.1.b), and the post-World War I division of German colonies and post-World War II changes in colonial policies (E.1).
Socialism and socialist ideology connect to the development of a Socialist state in Ethiopia under Mengistu (E.5.a).
Secularism and the separation of church and state connect to Ataturk's secularization reforms in Turkey (A.2).
HIV/AIDS as a global epidemic with geographic concentration in Southern and Eastern Africa connects to the AIDS pandemic in Botswana, Lesotho, and Swaziland (E.8.b.iii).
Geopolitics and resource competition connect to the strategic importance of petroleum in the Arabian Peninsula (B.4) and Iran's oil resources and geopolitical significance (C).

Economic and social development connect to the economic development and effects of colonialism on African peoples (E.2) and problems of economic development in independent West African nations (E.4.b.ii).
Capitalism and resource-based economies connect to the oil-based economic development of Saudi Arabia and the Arabian Peninsula (B.4.a).
Comparative analysis of global prosperity and poverty connects to colonial economic development in Africa (E.2) and economic challenges in West African nations (E.4).
Political borders and territorial disputes connect to Morocco's dispute over Western Sahara (D.2.c) and Namibia's contested borders and independence (E.8.b.ii).
Diaspora, migration, and transnational identity connect to the emergence of new educated African leaders and independence movements (E.4) shaped by diasporic intellectual networks.

Indigenous African religious traditions connect to the effects of colonialism on the societies and institutions of the African peoples (E.2), including the impact of Christianity and Islam on indigenous practices.
Military strategy including attrition and exhaustion connects to the Iran-Iraq war (C.1.c) as a major conflict requiring sustained military strategy.
Global poverty and development challenges connect to the economic effects of colonialism on African peoples (E.2) and problems of economic development in independent West African nations (E.4.b.ii).
